items.name: ClaudeCodeProvider.__init__.claude_path
items.reason: All 6 call sites (3 in tests, and none elsewhere in the codebase) invoke ClaudeCodeProvider() with no arguments, relying entirely on the default value of None. No caller ever passes claude_path explicitly. The parameter is used only in a short-circuit or-expression on line 59 (`resolved = claude_path or os.environ.get('OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH')`), meaning the claude_path branch of that expression is always falsy and the env-var fallback always takes effect.
items.remediation: Remove the claude_path parameter. Simplify line 59 to `resolved = os.environ.get('OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH')`. The OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH environment variable already provides the same override capability documented in the shadow doc, so no functionality is lost.

items.name: LiteLLMProvider.__init__.acompletion_fn
items.reason: All 11 deduplicated call sites (AnthropicProvider, GoogleProvider, OpenAIProvider, OpenRouterProvider — each calling super().__init__ with only a provider_name string) omit acompletion_fn entirely, relying on its default of None. No caller ever injects a custom completion function. The parameter appears designed as a test-injection escape hatch but is unused even in tests based on the complete call-site corpus provided.
items.remediation: Remove the acompletion_fn parameter and hardcode `self._acompletion = litellm.acompletion` directly. This eliminates the dead conditional branch on line 110.

items.name: JunkAnalyzer.analyze.on_progress
items.reason: All 7 of 7 deduplicated call sites are provided. Call sites 2–4 are subclass overrides of `analyze` (not invocations of the base class method), and call sites 5–7 are test fixture strings that write synthetic source code—none are actual invocations of `JunkAnalyzer.analyze` passing `on_progress`. No caller in the codebase passes this argument to the base class method. The parameter is forwarded unconditionally to `self.analyze_async(logging_provider, config, on_progress)`, so there are no conditional branches gated exclusively by it.
items.remediation: Remove the `on_progress` parameter from `JunkAnalyzer.analyze` and its forwarding in the `_run` coroutine. Also update the matching parameter in `analyze_async` and all subclass overrides if they are likewise never passed a value by their callers.

items.name: JunkAnalyzer.analyze.rate_limiter
items.reason: All 7 of 7 deduplicated call sites are provided. Call sites 2–4 are subclass overrides of `analyze` (not invocations of the base class method), and call sites 5–7 are test fixture strings—none are actual invocations of `JunkAnalyzer.analyze` passing `rate_limiter`. No caller in the codebase passes this argument to the base class method. The parameter is forwarded unconditionally to `create_runtime(config, rate_limiter=rate_limiter)`, so there are no conditional branches gated exclusively by it.
items.remediation: Remove the `rate_limiter` parameter from `JunkAnalyzer.analyze` and its forwarding to `create_runtime`. Also audit subclass overrides (junk_cicd.py, junk_deps.py, junk_orphan.py) and their callers in `audit.py` to determine whether `rate_limiter` is passed anywhere in the full call chain before removing it system-wide.

items.name: DeadDepsAnalyzer.analyze.on_progress
items.reason: All 3 deduplicated call sites never invoke `DeadDepsAnalyzer.analyze()` at all — two instantiate the class bare, one calls `analyze_async()` directly — so `on_progress` is never passed. However, confidence is reduced because the `JunkAnalyzer` ABC pattern (noted in project memory) suggests the audit orchestrator may call `analyzer.analyze(config, on_progress=...)` polymorphically, which would not be captured as a call to this specific override.
items.remediation: Verify whether `audit.py` (the orchestrator) passes `on_progress` via the `JunkAnalyzer` ABC interface. If not, remove the parameter and the corresponding positional argument passed to `self.analyze_async(logging_provider, config, on_progress)`.

items.name: DeadDepsAnalyzer.analyze.rate_limiter
items.reason: All 3 deduplicated call sites never invoke `DeadDepsAnalyzer.analyze()` at all — two instantiate the class bare, one calls `analyze_async()` directly — so `rate_limiter` is never passed. Confidence is reduced because the project memory explicitly states the JunkAnalyzer pattern is '`analyze()` wraps `analyze_async()` with `create_runtime(config, rate_limiter)`', implying the audit orchestrator likely passes `rate_limiter` through the ABC interface via polymorphic dispatch, which may not surface as a direct call to this override.
items.remediation: Verify whether `audit.py` passes `rate_limiter` via the `JunkAnalyzer` ABC interface. If not, remove the parameter and the `rate_limiter=rate_limiter` keyword argument in the `create_runtime(config, rate_limiter=rate_limiter)` call inside `_run()`.

items.name: gather_stats_async.use_api
items.reason: Both call sites explicitly pass `use_api=True`, which is identical to the default value. No caller ever passes `use_api=False`, meaning the deliberately-offline code path (skipping the `if use_api:` block and going straight to `_gather_file_stats_offline`) is never intentionally invoked. The offline path at lines ~238–243 remains reachable as an exception fallback when `use_api=True`, so it is not exclusively gated by the dead parameter and would not become dead code upon removal.
items.remediation: Remove the `use_api` parameter and inline the `if use_api:` guard (always-True branch). The sync wrapper `gather_stats()` already hardcodes `use_api=True` and the test at line 62 does the same. Dropping the parameter simplifies the API; the exception-fallback offline path at lines ~238–243 should be retained as it is reachable independently of `use_api`.

docs/explanatory/dead-code-detection.md.purpose: Dead code detection combining AST analysis and LLM verification for cross-file unused symbol identification.

findings.description: The architecture diagram places 'Transitive liveness (BFS) filters false positives' as a separate post-processing step after both the AST fast path and the Grep path (which is labeled as 'regex scan + LLM'). In reality, transitive liveness filtering happens *within* scan_references (before LLM verification) for the grep path, and within the AST fast path for AST-proven results. The diagram incorrectly implies transitive liveness occurs after LLM verification.
findings.evidence: scan_references(config, exclude_files=None, file_roles=None, facts_db=None) (L137-366) Pure Python, no LLM. ... Returns (zero_ref_candidates, low_ref_candidates) where: zero_ref_candidates: public symbols with 0 external file refs, after transitive-liveness filtering
findings.remediation: Restructure the diagram to show the correct pipeline order: regex scan → transitive liveness filtering → LLM verification → output. Transitive liveness should appear between the regex scanning step and the LLM verification step, not after both.

findings.description: The document states the grep path is 'implemented in the scan_references function' and then lists 5 steps including LLM verification as step 5. However, scan_references is explicitly 'Pure Python, no LLM' — it only implements steps 1-4 (regex scanning and candidate classification). The LLM verification (step 5) is implemented in _verify_batch_async and orchestrated by detect_dead_code_async, not scan_references.
findings.evidence: scan_references(config, exclude_files=None, file_roles=None, facts_db=None) (L137-366) Pure Python, no LLM.
findings.remediation: Clarify that the grep path spans multiple functions: scan_references handles the regex scanning and candidate identification (steps 1-4), while _verify_batch_async handles the LLM verification (step 5), orchestrated by detect_dead_code_async.

docs/explanatory/dead-plumbing-detection.purpose: Detect configuration fields that are defined but never actually enforced at runtime using semantic LLM analysis.

findings.description: The doc claims the shared `file_content_cache` dictionary avoids redundant file reads 'across obligations from the same schema,' but the shadow doc states it avoids redundant reads 'across all obligations.' The cache is shared globally across all obligations in Phase B, not scoped to obligations from the same schema.
findings.evidence: Calls `_find_field_references` to find all files containing the field name (regex `\bfieldName\b`), using a shared `file_content_cache` dict to avoid redundant file reads across all obligations.
findings.remediation: Change 'across obligations from the same schema' to 'across all obligations' to accurately reflect the cache scope.

docs/explanatory/facts-database-and-impo.purpose: Describes a facts database providing structured queries for import graphs, symbols, and cross-file references.

findings.description: The document claims observatory 'Uses `build_import_graph` via `_build_import_graph_edges`', implying that the observatory module's `_build_import_graph_edges` function delegates to the `FactsDB.build_import_graph()` method. In reality, `_build_import_graph_edges` builds its own edge list by calling lower-level FactsDB methods (`all_files()`, `get_file()`, `resolve_import_source()`) directly, and does NOT use `FactsDB.build_import_graph()`.
findings.evidence: _build_import_graph_edges(facts_db) (L152-176): Iterates facts_db.all_files(). Skips files where file_facts.classification is not None. Resolves import sources via facts_db.resolve_import_source. Deduplicates by (source, target) pair
findings.remediation: Change the observatory description to: 'Uses `_build_import_graph_edges` (which calls `all_files()`, `get_file()`, and `resolve_import_source()`) to emit the `import_graph` edge list in the export bundle.' Remove the misleading reference to `build_import_graph`.

docs/explanatory/junk-code-detection-fra.purpose: Explains Osoji's two-phase junk code detection framework combining static analysis with LLM verification.

findings.description: The document claims that DeadCICDAnalyzer's Phase 1 is purely static, listing only DeadPlumbingAnalyzer, DeadDepsAnalyzer, and OrphanedFilesAnalyzer as exceptions to the purely-static Phase 1 pattern. However, DeadCICDAnalyzer uses LLM calls in Phase 1 via _parse_cicd_via_llm() to parse Jenkinsfile, CircleCI, Azure Pipelines, and Travis CI config files. Four of the seven supported CI/CD file types are explicitly marked as 'LLM-parsed' in the shadow doc. DeadCICDAnalyzer should be listed as an exception alongside the other three.
findings.evidence: _parse_cicd_via_llm(provider, content, path, cicd_type, config) (L323-366) LLM fallback for unsupported types (Jenkinsfile, CircleCI, etc.). Uses ANTHROPIC_MODEL_SMALL / config.model_for("small"), tool extract_cicd_elements
findings.remediation: Add DeadCICDAnalyzer to the list of exceptions alongside DeadPlumbingAnalyzer, DeadDepsAnalyzer, and OrphanedFilesAnalyzer. Explain that DeadCICDAnalyzer uses LLM fallback in Phase 1 for CI/CD file types that lack regex-based parsers (Jenkinsfile, CircleCI, Azure Pipelines, Travis CI). Update the DeadCICDAnalyzer Phase 1 description to mention the LLM parsing fallback.

docs/explanatory/language-plugin-system..purpose: Documents Osoji's extensible language plugin system for AST-based fact extraction across multiple programming languages.

findings.description: The document describes `_detect_workspace_packages` under 'Monorepo support' as reading workspace configs to resolve internal package names, and the architecture diagram shows `workspacePackages` being sent to extract.js via stdin. This implies the data is actually used for cross-package import resolution during extraction. However, the shadow doc for extract.js explicitly states that workspacePackages is parsed but never used — it is silently discarded.
findings.evidence: ⚠️ `workspacePackages` is parsed but **never used** (field silently discarded after L145). ... `workspacePackages` field in the extended stdin format is accepted but completely ignored
findings.remediation: Add a note under the Monorepo support section or the TypeScript plugin description that `workspacePackages` is currently detected and sent to extract.js but not yet consumed by the runner — it is reserved for future use or is dead code.

findings.description: The document states that the LiteLLMProvider 'retries up to 3 times when the tool call output fails schema validation.' The shadow doc for litellm_provider.py defines _MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS = 3 as 'Max total API calls in tool-validation loop,' meaning 3 total attempts (1 initial + up to 2 retries), not 3 retries (which would be 4 total attempts).
findings.evidence: _MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS | 3 | Max total API calls in tool-validation loop
findings.remediation: Change 'retries up to 3 times' to 'makes up to 3 attempts' or 'retries up to 2 times' to accurately reflect _MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS = 3 total API calls.

findings.description: The 'File filtering' section's list of checked extensions omits `.mjs`, which is confirmed present in `CHECKABLE_EXTENSIONS`. The doc's own later 'Supported file types' table correctly includes `.mjs` under JavaScript/TypeScript, creating an internal inconsistency.
findings.evidence: CHECKABLE_EXTENSIONS (L9–45): set[str] of lowercase extensions that warrant safety inspection: Python (.py, .pyi), JS/TS (.js, .ts, .mjs, .jsx, .tsx)
findings.remediation: Add `.mjs` to the checked extensions list in the 'File filtering' subsection of Step 5, between `.ts` and `.jsx`, to match both the code and the doc's own later table.

docs/tutorials/using-doc-prompts.md.purpose: Teaches how to use Osoji's doc-prompts feature to identify and prioritize documentation gaps using concept-level analysis.

findings.description: The pipeline stages table labels Stage 4 ('Gap analysis + prompt generation') as type 'LLM', but Stage 4 actually consists of three sub-stages: 4a (_compute_priority) which is pure Python, 4b (_cluster_for_prompts) which is pure Python, and only 4c (_generate_writing_prompts_async) which uses LLM. Labeling the entire stage as 'LLM' is misleading since priority scoring and clustering are deterministic Python operations.
findings.evidence: Stage 4a — Priority scoring (`_compute_priority`, L334–381): Additive scoring... Stage 4b — Clustering (`_cluster_for_prompts`, L384–421): Groups underdocumented concepts... Stage 4c — Writing prompt generation (`_generate_writing_prompts_async`, L544–615): Single LLM call
findings.remediation: Change the Type column for Stage 4 from 'LLM' to 'Pure Python + LLM' to accurately reflect that priority scoring and clustering are pure Python while only prompt generation uses LLM.

concept_inventory.concept_description: The Click-based command-line interface that routes all user-facing subcommands (audit, shadow, diff, stats, hooks, safety, skills) to internal service modules, plus the package version declaration and bundled AI agent skill prompts exposed through the CLI.

Multi-source configuration resolution that merges CLI arguments, environment variables, TOML files, and built-in defaults into a Config dataclass that centralizes all .osoji/ filesystem path computations, provider/tier model mappings, and documentation-detection constants.

The 5.5-phase audit pipeline that sequences shadow checks, documentation analysis, code-debris verification, obligations, and junk analyzers into a unified AuditResult, then aggregates findings into a structured Scorecard with coverage, accuracy, junk density, and enforcement metrics.

The core Osoji capability: generating, caching, and managing LLM-produced shadow documentation files alongside source code, including staleness detection via SHA-256 hash headers, chunked large-file processing, plugin AST integration, sidecar metadata management, and per-file token statistics.

An in-memory database loaded from per-file .facts.json sidecar files that provides import graph traversal, cross-file symbol reference queries, export analysis, and string literal categorization for use by all analysis phases.
Git-aware source file enumeration with .osojiignore pattern matching, bottom-up depth sorting, and module-level caching — the shared foundation for all phases that need to iterate over project files.

Builds versioned JSON observatory export bundles aggregating shadow docs, audit findings, health metrics, import graphs, and doc analysis for downstream dashboards, and pushes them to the osoji-teams ingest API with git context and duplicate-commit detection.
The abstract LLMProvider base class, all shared message/tool/completion type primitives, and the barrel re-export module that together define the contract every concrete provider must implement.

Concrete LLM provider adapters for Anthropic (direct and Claude Code CLI), OpenAI, Google Gemini, and OpenRouter — all backed by LiteLLM with dual-format response parsing, forced-tool retry loops, and JSONL interaction logging.

Factory functions and registry that assemble the three-layer provider middleware stack (raw provider → rate-limited wrapper → logging wrapper) from Config, normalize model name strings, and manage provider metadata including API key env var names.

Reservation-based async rate limiter enforcing per-provider RPM and input/output TPM budgets, with adaptive output reservation, retry-with-backoff, header-driven auto-tuning, and provider-aware exact/offline token counting.
All forced-output tool JSON Schemas used across audit phases, plus the lightweight recursive JSON Schema validator that produces human-readable dot-path error messages for LLM tool call inputs.

A two-part pipeline: first, doc_analysis classifies and accuracy-validates documentation files against shadow docs using a 4-tier LLM pipeline with Diataxis categorization; second, doc_prompts clusters source files into documentable concepts, maps coverage gaps, scores priorities, and generates targeted LLM writing prompts.
The shared JunkAnalyzer abstract base class, JunkFinding data container, async/sync bridge, and shared LLM verification utilities that all concrete junk-code detectors inherit from and build upon.

Five concrete junk analyzers — dead code (AST fast-path + grep + LLM verification), dead parameters (call-site scanning), dead dependencies (multi-ecosystem manifest parsing), dead CI/CD elements (regex pipeline parsing + path validation), and orphaned files (import graph BFS + LLM entry-point identification) — all implementing the JunkAnalyzer ABC.

Two related detectors targeting invisible coupling: obligations.py finds cross-file string contract violations (string literals shared between producers, checkers, and definers), while plumbing.py finds config schema fields that are parsed but never reach a runtime actuator enforcing their declared behavioral obligation.
Classifies git-changed files to identify stale shadow docs and documentation references affected by a commit or PR, producing a DiffImpactReport with a config snapshot for CI integration.

The LanguagePlugin abstract base class, ExtractedFacts data container, module-level singleton registry with entry-point-based auto-discovery, and bootstrap initialization — the extension framework that maps file extensions to language-specific AST fact extractors.

The first-party Python plugin (stdlib ast, two-pass extraction) and TypeScript plugin (spawning a ts-morph Node.js subprocess with monorepo and multi-tsconfig support) that extract imports, exports, calls, member_writes, and string_literals from project source files.

A composable safety subsystem that scans staged or arbitrary files for accidental personal filesystem path leaks and secrets, using pre-compiled regex patterns and an optional detect-secrets integration, with structured CheckResult aggregation and human-readable reporting.
Installs, uninstalls, and manages Osoji git hooks (pre-commit, post-commit, pre-push) across standard repos, worktrees, and submodules, with staged file enumeration for safety gate integration.
A bounded-concurrency async task runner that limits in-flight coroutines to a configurable cap via a sliding-window mechanism, used by all high-fanout analysis phases to prevent resource exhaustion.

Shared pytest fixtures (temp_dir lifecycle, git ls-files cache isolation, baseline regression flag), a one-sided binomial test framework for statistically detecting LLM prompt regressions with controlled alpha and power, and the prompt regression test runner that executes real LLM trials against snapshotted fixtures.

## TASK
Write a **reference document** covering the Osoji test infrastructure: shared pytest fixtures (conftest.py), statistical utilities for LLM regression testing (stat_utils.py), and the prompt regression test runner (test_prompt_regression.py). This document must be a complete, scannable reference that a contributor can consult while writing or debugging tests — not a tutorial or guide.

---

## AUDIENCE
Osoji contributors writing new tests or diagnosing test failures. They understand Python and pytest but may be unfamiliar with the project's LLM-specific testing patterns. They will consult this document when they need precise details: fixture names, parameter types, return values, CLI flags, and statistical guarantees.

---

## SCOPE

### In scope — document every item below with full precision:

**`tests/conftest.py` — Shared Fixtures and Hooks**
- `pytest_addoption(parser)` — plugin hook that registers the `--establish-baseline` CLI flag; document the flag name, type (bool store_true), and what mode it activates (baseline-writing vs. comparison)
- `establish_baseline(request) -> bool` — fixture returning the resolved value of `--establish-baseline`; document scope, how to declare it as a test parameter, and what the bool controls downstream
- `_clear_repo_files_cache()` — autouse session/function-scoped fixture; document that it calls `clear_repo_files_cache()` from `osoji.walker` both before (setup) and after (teardown) every test; document the cache-pollution problem it solves and why it is autouse
- Note the dual fixture convention: project uses both `temp_dir` (project fixture) and `tmp_path` (pytest built-in); document when each appears and any behavioral differences relevant to test authors

**`tests/stat_utils.py` — Binomial Regression Test Framework**
- Module-level constants: `_RELATIVE_DROP = 0.4` (40% relative drop defines a detectable regression), `_ALPHA = 0.01` (1% significance level for both test rejection and confidence intervals); document that these are private but govern all public function behavior
- `compute_sample_size(p0: float) -> int`:
  - Parameters: `p0` — baseline pass rate (float in (0, 1])
  - Return value: minimum number of LLM trials N required to detect a `_RELATIVE_DROP` relative regression from `p0` at significance `_ALPHA` with adequate statistical power
  - Algorithm summary: describe the one-sided binomial power calculation at a conceptual level (no need to reproduce the math, but name the inputs: p0, p1 = p0*(1-_RELATIVE_DROP), alpha, power target)
  - Edge cases / assumptions: what happens at boundary values of p0 (e.g., p0=1.0, p0 very small); any exceptions raised
- Document the second public function (visible in test_prompt_regression.py or stat_utils.py) responsible for performing the actual binomial test given observed trial results; include: parameters (observed passes, N, p0), return type, what the return value means (reject / fail-to-reject, p-value, or confidence bound), and how callers should interpret it

**`tests/test_prompt_regression.py` — Prompt Regression Test Runner**
- Overall pattern: how the runner executes real LLM trials against snapshotted fixtures; what a "trial" means (one LLM call, pass/fail outcome)
- Baseline mode vs. comparison mode: what `--establish-baseline` causes the runner to do differently (write snapshot vs. compare against snapshot); where snapshots are stored
- Any public helpers or fixtures exported from this file that other test modules may import
- How `establish_baseline` fixture and `compute_sample_size` wire together in a typical test

**`tests/__init__.py`**
- Document any symbols exported (if the file is non-empty / has exports); if it is an empty package marker, state that explicitly

### Out of scope — reference but do not explain:
- The statistical theory behind one-sided binomial tests (link to a standard reference if helpful, but do not re-derive)
- Osoji's audit phases, junk analyzers, or facts DB — mention only if a fixture directly touches them
- How to *write* a new prompt regression test (that belongs in a how-to guide)
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` content — do not duplicate; instead cross-reference it with a note like "For project setup and general contribution workflow, see CONTRIBUTING.md"

---

## QUALITY CRITERIA
This document is "done" when:
1. **Complete API surface**: every public fixture, function, constant, and CLI flag is documented with name, type signature, parameters, return value, and behavior — no public symbol omitted
2. **Scannable structure**: uses a consistent heading hierarchy (H2 per module, H3 per symbol); includes a summary table of fixtures and a summary table of stat_utils symbols near the top for quick lookup
3. **Precise parameter documentation**: every parameter has its type, valid range, and what happens at boundaries or with invalid input
4. **Error conditions**: any exceptions raised (or explicitly "raises nothing; returns…") are documented for each function
5. **Autouse behavior is explicit**: the reference states clearly that `_clear_repo_files_cache` runs around *every* test automatically so contributors do not need to declare it
6. **Statistical guarantees are stated, not derived**: document the false-positive rate (1%), relative regression magnitude detectable (40%), and power level without reproducing proofs
7. **No tutorial prose**: avoid phrases like "first do X, then do Y" or motivational framing; use declarative, present-tense statements ("Returns the minimum N…", "Calls clear_repo_files_cache() before and after…")
8. **Cross-references are accurate**: CONTRIBUTING.md is referenced, not duplicated

---

## CONSISTENCY GUIDANCE
- **CONTRIBUTING.md** is the only existing doc covering this concept space. Skim it before writing to ensure you do not duplicate setup instructions or conflict with any conventions stated there. This reference document complements CONTRIBUTING.md by providing API-level precision that CONTRIBUTING.md lacks.
- Match any terminology already established in CONTRIBUTING.md (e.g., if it names the baseline flag differently, use that name).
- The project uses `temp_dir` as its primary test fixture name (defined in conftest.py); use that name consistently rather than `tmp_path` unless specifically contrasting the two.
- Fixture and function names must match the source exactly — do not paraphrase or rename symbols in the documentation.
- If the project has an existing docs directory with other reference files, match their heading style, table format, and front-matter conventions exactly.

## `tests/conftest.py` — Shared pytest fixtures

### Purpose
Provides shared pytest fixtures and CLI option extensions for all osoji tests. Autouse fixture ensures `clear_repo_files_cache()` runs before and after every test to prevent cache pollution across tests.

### Fixtures & Hooks

**`pytest_addoption` (L12-18)** — Pytest plugin hook that registers `--establish-baseline` CLI flag. When passed, prompt regression tests run in baseline-writing mode instead of comparison mode.

**`establish_baseline` (L22-23)** — Fixture returning a bool; resolves `--establish-baseline` from the parsed CLI options via `request.config.getoption()`. Tests needing this flag declare it as a parameter.

**`_clear_repo_files_cache` (L27-31)** — `autouse=True` fixture that calls `clear_repo_files_cache()` (imported from `osoji.walker`) both before the test (`yield`) and after. Prevents `git ls-files` cache state from leaking between tests. The leading underscore signals it is infrastructure, not consumer-facing.

**`temp_dir` (L35-39)** — Yields a `pathlib.Path` pointing to a freshly-created OS temporary directory. Cleans up with `shutil.rmtree(..., ignore_errors=True)` after the test, even on failure. Project convention: use `temp_dir` (not pytest's built-in `tmp_path`) for this project's tests, though both are technically available.

### Key Dependencies
- `osoji.walker.clear_repo_files_cache` — called before and after every test automatically.
- `pytest` — `@pytest.fixture`, `pytest_addoption` hook, `request` proxy.
- `pathlib.Path`, `tempfile.mkdtemp`, `shutil.rmtree` — temp directory lifecycle management.

### Architectural Notes
- `_clear_repo_files_cache` is `autouse=True` with function scope (default), meaning it wraps every individual test automatically — no opt-in required.
- `temp_dir` uses `tempfile.mkdtemp()` + manual cleanup (not `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`) to

## `tests/stat_utils.py` — Statistical Utilities for Prompt Regression Tests

### Purpose
Provides a one-sided binomial test framework for detecting regressions in stochastic LLM-based tests with controlled false-positive and false-negative rates. Consumed by test files that run multiple LLM prompt trials and need to assert consistent pass rates.

---

### Module-Level Constants (L9–L10)
| Name | Value | Meaning |
|------|-------|---------|
| `_RELATIVE_DROP` | `0.4` | A 40% relative drop in pass rate constitutes a detectable regression |
| `_ALPHA` | `0.01` | Significance level — 1% false-positive rate for both test rejection and CI |

Both constants are private (`_`-prefixed) but implicitly shared across the two public functions.

---

### `compute_sample_size(p0: float) -> int` (L13–38)
Determines the **minimum trial count N** needed to detect a 40% relative regression with 99% power at 1% alpha.

**Algorithm**: Iterates `n` from 5 to 70. For each candidate `n`, computes the empirical rejection probability under `H1: p = p0` by summing `P(X=k | p0) * I(binomtest(k, n, threshold) rejects H0)` over all `k`. Returns the first `n` where this reaches `power=0.99`. Falls back to `max_n=70` if no qualifying `n` is found.

- **Null hypothesis boundary**: `threshold = p0 * (1 - 0.4)` = `0.6 * p0`
- **Test direction**: `alternative="greater"` — rejects when observed rate significantly exceeds `threshold`
- **Typical output range**: 10–68 trials depending on `p0`; hard cap at 70

**Key calls**: `binom.pmf(k, n, p0)` (L32), `binomtest(k, n, threshold, alternative="greater")` (L33)

---

### `assert_pass_rate(k: int, n: int, p0: float) -> None` (L41–58)
**Assertion entry point** for test files. Raises `AssertionError` if the observed `k/n` pass rate cannot statistically demonstrate it exceeds the regression threshold.

## Prompt Regression Test Suite

This file implements prompt regression tests that verify LLM prompt changes don't introduce regressions on known edge cases. Tests make real API calls via the Anthropic provider and are gated behind the `prompt_regression` pytest marker. Two operational modes: **baseline establishment** (30 trials → write p0 to `expected.json`) and **statistical verification** (compute N via binomial test, assert pass rate).

---

### Fixture Layout

All fixtures live under `tests/fixtures/prompt_regression/` (L32), organized as:
- `dead_code/case_NNN_*/` — dead code detection cases
- `dead_params/case_NNN_*/` — dead parameter detection cases
- `plumbing/case_NNN_*/` — obligation extraction cases
- `latent_bug/case_NNN_*/` — latent bug detection cases

Each case directory contains: `source/` (snapshotted Python/TS files), optionally `symbols/` and `facts/`, and `expected.json` (ground truth + optional `baseline.p0`).

---

### `_setup_case_dir(tmp_path, case_dir)` (L35–67)
Copies case fixture files into `tmp_path`, mirrors `.osoji/symbols/` and `.osoji/facts/` subdirectories, returns `Config(root_path=tmp_path, respect_gitignore=False)`. Used by every test case.

---

### Deterministic Tests (No LLM)

**`test_dead_params_002_high_fanout_fixture_limits_callers`** (L70–84): Calls `scan_dead_param_candidates(config)`, finds the target candidate by `function_name`/`param_name` from `expected.json`, asserts the sorted set of `call_site.file_path` matches `expected["call_site_files"]`. Verifies that high-fanout common-name functions only retain direct importer call sites.

**`test_plumbing_002_doc_json_fixture_stays_doc_only`** (L87–94): Calls `config.is_doc_candidate()` and `load_files_by_role(config, "schema")` and asserts expected values. Verifies doc JSON files stay out of schema role classification.

---

### Trial Runner Fun

## Project Root — Osoji Shadow Documentation Engine

### Overview

The project root is the top-level layout for **Osoji** (`osojicode` on PyPI, `osoji` as the importable package) — a multi-phase, LLM-powered shadow documentation and audit engine designed to generate semantically dense codebase summaries for AI agent consumption. The root contains:

- **`.osoji.toml`** — runtime configuration consumed by the Osoji toolchain itself
- **`pyproject.toml`** — build, packaging, and dev tooling configuration
- **`src/`** — sole application source tree (`src/osoji/` package)
- **`tests/`** — complete pytest test suite mirroring every production module

---

### Component Roles and Relationships

#### Configuration Layer
`.osoji.toml` provides the single runtime knob at the project level: the `[push].endpoint` URL (`https://api.osojicode.ai`) used when uploading audit findings or shadow docs. It is discovered by convention (working directory / tree walk) and carries no secrets. `pyproject.toml` configures the build backend (Hatchling), CLI entry point (`osoji → osoji.cli:main`), runtime dependencies (`anthropic`, `click`, `litellm`, `tabulate`), optional extras (`detect-secrets` for safety features), and pytest settings (`asyncio_mode = "strict"`, custom markers `prompt_regression` / `live_smoke`).

#### Application Package (`src/osoji/`)
All production logic lives in `src/osoji/`. Key subsystems:

| Subsystem | Module(s) | Role |
|---|---|---|
| CLI | `cli.py` | Click group exposing `shadow`, `audit`, `diff`, `stats`, `push`, `hooks`, `safety`, `skills` |
| Audit orchestration | `audit.py` | 5.5-phase pipeline: shadow staleness, doc accuracy, code debris (2-phase w/ LLM verification), cross-file obligations, junk analyzers |
| Shadow doc generation | `shadow.py` | Per-file and per-directory Markdown summaries written to `.osoji/shadow/` |
| LLM abstraction | `llm/` | Three-layer provider stack: Logging → RateLimited → LiteLLM/ClaudeCode; `create_runtime(config)` is the factory |
| Facts database | `facts.py` | Aggregates `.facts.json` sidecars into a cross-file reference graph; powers dead-code evidence |
| Plugin-based AST extraction | `plugins/` | Extracts structured facts from Python and TypeScript source files |
| Junk analyzers | `junk.py` + siblings | `JunkAnalyzer` ABC; dead code, dead params, dead deps, dead CI/CD, orphaned files |
| Config | `config.py` | Central `Config` object; all path logic flows through it |
| Safety | `safety/` | Pre-commit secret/path detection |
| Skills | `skills/` | Filesystem-backed AI agent skill prompts |

Data flow: CLI invokes `run_audit` / `generate_shadow_docs` → `Config` resolves all paths → async phases dispatch LLM calls via `create_runtime` → findings/shadow docs written under `.osoji/` → `push` uploads envelope to `.osoji.toml`-configured endpoint.

#### Test Suite (`tests/`)
The test suite provides near-complete coverage of every production module. It follows strict conventions:
- `temp_dir` fixture (from `conftest.py`) is the canonical temporary directory — not pytest's `tmp_path`.
- LLM calls are universally mocked via `AsyncMock` + `CompletionResult` / `ToolCall` objects.
- `stat_utils.py` provides a binomial pass-rate framework for stochastic prompt regression tests.
- Internal/private functions are imported and tested directly for critical algorithmic paths.
- Async tests are decorated `@pytest.mark.asyncio`; `asyncio_mode = "strict"` is enforced globally.

---

### Primary Entry Points

| Symbol | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `main` | `src/osoji/cli.py` (→ `osoji` console script) | Top-level CLI; all user-facing commands |
| `run_audit` / `run_audit_async` | `src/osoji/audit.py` | Synchronous/async audit orchestrator |
| `generate_shadow_docs` / `generate_shadow_docs_async` | `src/osoji/shadow.py` | Shadow doc generation pipeline |
| `create_runtime` | `src/osoji/llm/runtime.py` | LLM provider stack factory |
| `FactsDB` | `src/osoji/facts.py` | Cross-file reference index |

---

### Key Conventions

- **`src/` layout**: the importable module is `osoji`; the pip-installable package is `osojicode`.
- **No hardcoded paths**: everything resolves through `Config`.
- **Concurrency**: `gather_with_buffer` in `async_utils.py` manages concurrent async phases.
- **Tool definitions**: centralized in `tools.py`, converted by `_dict_to_tool_definition()`.
- **Output artifacts**: all written under `.osoji/` (shadow docs, facts, findings, symbols, signatures).
- **Optional dependencies**: `detect-secrets` is NOT available unless `safety` or `all` extra is installed; `scipy`/`jsonschema` are dev-only.
- **Push credentials**: never stored in `.osoji.toml`; expected from environment variables.

## `src` — Python Source Root

The `src` directory is the Python source root for the Osoji project. It contains a single top-level package, `osoji`, which constitutes the entire application: a multi-phase documentation audit and shadow-doc generation engine driven by a Click CLI.

---

## Structure

```
src/
└── osoji/          # Sole package — all application logic lives here
```

There are no sibling packages, shared utilities, or other modules at the `src` level. All imports, entry points, and public APIs are rooted at `osoji`.

---

## Package Summary: `osoji`

The `osoji` package implements a 5.5-phase audit pipeline and LLM-powered shadow documentation generator. Its responsibilities span:

- **Shadow doc generation** — per-file and per-directory Markdown summaries written to `.osoji/shadow/` via `shadow.py` and `generate_directory_shadows`.
- **Audit orchestration** — `audit.py` coordinates five concurrent phases: staleness check, doc accuracy analysis, dead-code debris verification, cross-file obligation extraction, and junk analysis (dead code, dead params, dead deps, dead CI/CD, orphaned files).
- **LLM abstraction** — `llm/` provides a three-layer provider stack (logging → rate-limited → LiteLLM/ClaudeCode) consumed by every async phase via `create_runtime(config)`.
- **Facts database** — `facts.py` aggregates `.facts.json` sidecars into an in-memory cross-file reference graph, powering dead-code evidence queries.
- **Plugin-based AST extraction** — `plugins/` extracts structured facts from Python and TypeScript source files.
- **CLI surface** — `cli.py` exposes `shadow`, `audit`, `diff`, `stats`, `push`, `hooks`, `safety`, and `skills` subcommands via a Click group.
- **Safety and skills sub-packages** — pre-commit secret/path detection (`safety/`) and filesystem-backed AI agent skill prompts (`skills/`).

---

## Key Entry Points

| Symbol | Module | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `main` | `osoji/cli.py` | Click CLI group; primary user-facing entry point |
| `run_audit` / `run_audit_async` | `osoji/audit.py` | Synchronous/async audit orchestrator |
| `generate_shadow_docs` / `generate_shadow_docs_async` | `osoji/shadow.py` | Shadow doc generation pipeline |
| `create_runtime` | `osoji/llm/runtime.py` | LLM provider stack factory |
| `FactsDB` | `osoji/facts.py` | Cross-file reference index |

---

## Conventions

- **All path logic** flows through `Config` (`osoji/config.py`); no module hardcodes paths.
- **Concurrency** is managed via `gather_with_buffer` (`osoji/async_utils.py`) with lambda factories.
- **Tool definitions** for LLM dispatch are centralized in `osoji/tools.py`.
- **Junk analyzers** all subclass `JunkAnalyzer` ABC from `osoji/junk.py` and follow the same sync-wraps-async pattern.
- **Output artifacts** are written under `.osoji/` (shadow docs, facts, findings, symbols, signatures, analysis results).

---

## Relationship to Broader Project

`src/` is the `pyproject.toml`-declared source layout root. The `osoji` package is installed as the `osoji` console script. All test fixtures, configuration files, and runtime outputs live outside `src/`; the package itself is pure application logic with no internal coupling to test infrastructure.

## `src/osoji` — Core Osoji Shadow Documentation Engine

The `osoji` package is a multi-phase documentation audit and shadow-doc generation system. It generates LLM-powered "shadow documentation" for every source file in a codebase, then runs a 5.5-phase audit pipeline to detect stale docs, dead code, unused dependencies, config field violations, cross-file contract fragility, and documentation gaps—all coordinated by a single audit orchestrator and surfaced through a Click CLI.

---

## Package-Level Entry Points

| Symbol | Module | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `main` (Click group) | `cli.py` | Primary CLI entry point (`osoji shadow`, `audit`, `diff`, `stats`, `push`, `hooks`, `safety`, `skills`) |
| `run_audit` / `run_audit_async` | `audit.py` | Synchronous / async audit orchestrator; returns `AuditResult` |
| `generate_shadow_docs` / `generate_shadow_docs_async` | `shadow.py` | Shadow doc generation pipeline |
| `check_shadow_docs` / `mark_stale_docs` | `shadow.py` | Staleness detection and stale-warning injection |
| `build_observatory_bundle` / `write_observatory_bundle` | `observatory.py` | Versioned export bundle assembly |
| `run_push` | `push.py` | Push observatory bundle to osoji-teams ingest API |
| `run_diff` | `diff.py` | Git-diff documentation impact analysis |
| `create_runtime` | `llm/runtime.py` | LLM provider stack factory (used by all async analysis phases) |

---

## Architecture Overview

```
CLI (cli.py)
    │
    ├── osoji shadow .  →  shadow.py  [shadow doc generation]
    │                        ├── walker.py           [file discovery]
    │                        ├── hasher.py            [staleness/hash]
    │                        ├── plugins/             [AST extraction]
    │                        ├── llm/                 [LLM provider stack]
    │                        └── facts.py             [writes .facts.json]
    │
    └── osoji audit  →  audit.py  [5.5-phase orchestrator]
                          │
                          ├── Phase 1: shadow.py          [stale shadow check/fix]
                          ├── Phase 2: doc_analysis.py    [doc accuracy + Diataxis classification]
                          ├── Phase 3: debris verify       [cross-file dead-code evidence]
                          │           ├── facts.py         [cross_file_references()]
                          │           └── deadcode.py, deadparam.py
                          ├── Phase 3.5: obligations.py   [cross-file string contracts]
                          ├── Phase 4: junk analyzers      [concurrent]
                          │           ├── deadcode.py      [DeadCodeAnalyzer]
                          │           ├── deadparam.py     [DeadParameterAnalyzer]
                          │           ├── plumbing.py      [DeadPlumbingAnalyzer]
                          │           ├── junk_deps.py     [DeadDepsAnalyzer]
                          │           ├── junk_cicd.py     [DeadCICDAnalyzer]
                          │           └── junk_orphan.py   [OrphanedFilesAnalyzer]
                          ├── Phase 5: scorecard.py        [metrics aggregation]
                          └── Phase 5.5: doc_prompts.py   [writing prompt generation]
```

Phases 2, 3, 3.5, and 4 execute **concurrently** via a single `asyncio.gather` call in `run_audit_async`.

---

## Key Data Flow

### Shadow Doc Generation (`shadow.py`)
1. `walker.py` discovers all source files (git-aware via `list_repo_files`).
2. `plugins/` performs per-file AST extraction (Python, TypeScript) → `ExtractedFacts`.
3. `hasher.py` computes source + impl hashes; stale files trigger LLM regeneration.
4. Per-file: `generate_file_shadow_doc_async` forces the `submit_shadow_doc` tool; writes `.shadow.md`, `.findings.json`, `.symbols.json`, `.facts.json`, `.signatures.json` sidecars.
5. `generate_directory_shadows` runs bottom-up (leaf-to-root) to produce `_directory.shadow.md` roll-ups.
6. `extract_doc_references` writes `.facts.json` for doc files recording source references.

### Audit Pipeline (`audit.py`)
- `analysis_root` (`.osoji/analysis/`) is wiped and recreated at run start — no stale accumulation.
- A single `RateLimiter` (from `rate_limiter.py`) is shared across all phases to track cumulative token spend.
- Phase 3 debris verification is best-effort (failures silently retain all findings).
- `AuditResult` is serialized as `audit-result.json`; `load_audit_result` reconstructs it for the `report` subcommand.

### Facts Database (`facts.py`)
`FactsDB` loads all `.facts.json` sidecars into memory at construction time. It provides:
- `cross_file_references(symbol, source_file)` — primary dead-code evidence query
- `importers_of(source_path)` — "who calls this file?"
- `docs_referencing(source_path)` — doc-to-source linkage
- `build_import_graph()` — full project import adjacency

### Junk Analyzer Pattern (`junk.py`)
All junk analyzers subclass `JunkAnalyzer` ABC:
1. `analyze()` (sync): creates a fresh `create_runtime` provider, runs `asyncio.run(analyze_async(...))`, closes provider.
2. `analyze_async()`: cheap Python candidate scan → bounded parallel LLM verification via `gather_with_buffer`.
3. Returns `JunkAnalysisResult(findings, total_candidates, analyzer_name)`.
4. `JUNK_ANALYZERS` list in `audit.py` is the authoritative registry.

---

## Configuration Layer (`config.py`)

`Config` is the single source of truth for all path computations and LLM model resolution. All modules receive `config` as a parameter; none hardcode paths.

- **Resolution order**: `cli` → `env` → `project (.osoji.local.toml)` → `global (~/.config/osoji/config.toml)` → `builtin`
- **Model tiers**: `"small"` / `"medium"` / `"large"` resolved via `config.model_for(tier)`
- **Key path helpers**: `shadow_path_for(path)`, `findings_path_for(path)`, `symbols_path_for(path)`, `facts_path_for(path)`, `analysis_root`, `shadow_root`

---

## LLM Abstraction (`llm/`)

A three-layer provider stack assembled by `create_runtime(config)`:
```
LoggingProvider (stats + verbose output)
    └── RateLimitedProvider (RPM/TPM reservation tickets)
            └── LiteLLMProvider / ClaudeCodeProvider (HTTP or subprocess)
```

All callers use `CompletionOptions(model=..., tools=[...], reservation_key="phase.name")` and receive `CompletionResult`. Tool validation retry (up to 3 attempts with LLM self-correction) is built into concrete providers.

---

## Tool Definitions (`tools.py`)

Central registry of all LLM tool schemas. Pattern: `UPPERCASE_DICT_TOOL` constant → `get_*_tool_definitions() -> list[ToolDefinition]` factory. Tool name strings are LLM dispatch keys — must match `tool_call.name` in all consumers. Key tools by phase:

| Phase | Key Tools |
|---|---|
| Shadow gen | `submit_shadow_doc`, `submit_directory_shadow_doc` |
| Doc analysis | `match_doc_topics`, `analyze_document`, `verify_doc_finding` |
| Dead code | `verify_dead_code`, `verify_dead_parameters`, `verify_debris_findings` |
| Junk | `verify_dead_deps`, `verify_dead_cicd`, `verify_orphan_files`, `extract_obligations`, `verify_actuation` |
| Doc prompts | `build_concept_inventory`, `generate_writing_prompts` |

---

## Concurrency Pattern

`async_utils.gather_with_buffer(factories, max_pending=256)` is the universal concurrency primitive used by all high-fanout phases. It implements a sliding-window task cap to prevent rate-limit or memory pressure. **All callers pass lambda factories, not bare coroutines**, to defer task creation until the window permits.

---

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

- **Staleness detection**: `hasher.py` computes 16-char truncated SHA-256 hashes of source content (`@source-hash`) and the Osoji implementation itself (`@impl-hash`, cached via `@lru_cache`). Both must match for a shadow doc to be considered fresh.
- **Ignore patterns**: `walker._matches_ignore()` implements fnmatch-based gitignore-style filtering applied consistently across `walker`, `diff`, `doc_analysis`, and all junk analyzers.
- **Error resilience**: All batch async operations catch per-item exceptions and continue; Phase 3 is wrapped in a global try/except that preserves all findings on LLM failure.
- **Token accounting**: Every async phase captures `(input_tokens, output_tokens)` from `logging_provider.stats`; a summary table is printed at audit end when multiple phases consumed tokens.

---

## Sub-Packages

| Sub-package | Role |
|---|---|
| `llm/` | Provider abstraction, rate limiting, token counting, tool-call validation |
| `plugins/` | Language-specific AST extraction (Python stdlib, TypeScript via Node.js); feeds FactsDB |
| `safety/` | Pre-commit personal path and secret detection; optional `detect-secrets` integration |
| `skills/` | Filesystem-backed registry of Markdown AI agent skill prompts |

---

## Output Artifacts (`.osoji/` directory)

| Artifact | Written by | Read by |
|---|---|---|
| `.osoji/shadow/**/*.shadow.md` | `shadow.py` | `audit.py`, `doc_analysis.py`, all junk analyzers |
| `.osoji/facts/**/*.facts.json` | `shadow.py`, `extract_doc_references` | `facts.py` (FactsDB) |
| `.osoji/symbols/**/*.symbols.json` | `shadow.py` | `symbols.py`, dead code/param analyzers |
| `.osoji/findings/**/*.findings.json` | `shadow.py` | `audit.py` Phase 3, `scorecard.py`, `observatory.py` |
| `.osoji/signatures/**/*.signature.json` | `shadow.py` | `doc_prompts.py`, `junk_orphan.py`, `observatory.py` |
| `.osoji/analysis/audit-result.json` | `audit.py` | `cli.py report`, `observatory.py`, `push.py` |
| `.osoji/analysis/scorecard.json` | `scorecard.py` | `observatory.py` |
| `.osoji/analysis/observatory.json` | `observatory.py` | `push.py` |
| `.osoji/staleness.json` | `shadow.mark_stale_docs` | `cli.py check` |
| `.osoji/token-cache.json` | `stats.py` | `stats.py`, `observatory.py` |

## `src/osoji/llm` — LLM Provider Abstraction Package

### Overall Purpose

This package provides a unified, provider-agnostic interface for making LLM completions throughout the Osoji audit system. It handles the full lifecycle of LLM interaction: provider instantiation, rate limiting, token accounting, interaction logging, tool call validation/retry, and token budget enforcement. All consumers (junk analyzers, audit phases) interact with a single `LLMProvider` interface regardless of the underlying backend.

---

### Architecture Overview

The package is organized into four layers:

```
[Caller / JunkAnalyzer.analyze()]
        ↓ create_runtime(config)
[LoggingProvider]          ← outermost decorator; stats + verbose output
[RateLimitedProvider]      ← reservation/ticket-based RPM/TPM enforcement
[Concrete Provider]        ← LiteLLMProvider (HTTP) or ClaudeCodeProvider (CLI)
        ↓
[LLM API / claude CLI subprocess]
```

#### 1. Type Layer (`types.py`)
All shared contracts live here. Key types:
- **`Message` / `MessageRole`** — conversation primitives
- **`ToolDefinition` / `ToolCall`** — tool-calling contract
- **`CompletionOptions`** — full request parameter bag (model, tokens, tools, validators, reservation key, budget guard)
- **`CompletionResult`** — structured response (content, tool_calls, token counts, stop_reason, rate_limit metadata, response headers)
- **`PromptTooLargeError`, `ToolSchemaValidationError`, `RequiredToolCallError`** — structured exceptions with retry context

#### 2. Abstract Base + Concrete Providers
- **`base.py`** — `LLMProvider` ABC: `name` property, `complete(messages, system, options)`, `close()`
- **`litellm_provider.py`** — `LiteLLMProvider`: the shared HTTP implementation backing Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, OpenRouter. Features tool-validation retry loops (up to 3 attempts), dual response-format parsing (Anthropic-native vs. OpenAI wire format), JSONL interaction logging, and input token budget enforcement.
- **`anthropic.py`, `google.py`, `openai.py`, `openrouter.py`** — Zero-logic subclasses of `LiteLLMProvider`, each passing a provider discriminant string (`"anthropic"`, `"google"`, `"openai"`, `"openrouter"`) to the parent constructor.
- **`claude_code.py`** — `ClaudeCodeProvider`: standalone subprocess-based provider that invokes the `claude -p` CLI. Strips `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from the subprocess environment (forces OAuth/subscription auth). Supports structured-output JSON via `--json-schema` flag and implements its own tool-validation retry loop. `stop_reason` is always hardcoded to `"end_turn"`.

#### 3. Decorator Providers
- **`rate_limited.py`** — `RateLimitedProvider`: wraps any `LLMProvider` with proactive RPM/TPM reservation via a ticket/acquire/finalize pattern. Three-tier input token estimation (explicit override → `TokenCounter` → offline heuristic). Auto-tunes rate limits from response headers (Anthropic/OpenAI header formats). Retries retryable errors (rate limits, 5xx, connection errors) up to 3 times with exponential backoff + `retry-after` header parsing.
- **`logging.py`** — `LoggingProvider`: wraps any `LLMProvider` to accumulate `TokenStats` (input/output tokens, request count, length-stop events). Optionally prints verbose `[tokens]` lines per call. `get_token_summary()` produces a final usage report with top-3 length-stop examples and rate-limit summary (duck-typed via `get_rate_limit_summary()`).

#### 4. Support Modules
- **`registry.py`** — `ProviderSpec` frozen dataclass + `_PROVIDER_SPECS` dict. Public API: `normalize_provider_name()`, `get_provider_spec()`, `provider_names()`, `qualify_model_name()`, `strip_provider_prefix()`. Defines the canonical provider list, LiteLLM prefixes, API key env vars, and rate-limit bucket names.
- **`factory.py`** — `create_provider(name)` (bare provider) and `create_logging_provider(name, *, rate_limiter, verbose, default_model)` (fully-decorated stack without `create_runtime`'s `Config` dependency).
- **`runtime.py`** — `create_runtime(config, *, verbose, rate_limiter) → (LoggingProvider, RateLimiter)`. The primary integration point used by all `JunkAnalyzer.analyze()` / `analyze_async()` callers. Assembles the full three-layer stack from a `Config` object; accepts an injectable `rate_limiter` for sharing across invocations.
- **`tokens.py`** — `TokenCounter`: async token counting via Anthropic `count_tokens` API (for Anthropic) or LiteLLM tokenizer (for others). Text-level caching (10k entries). `estimate_tokens_offline(text)` and `estimate_completion_input_tokens_offline(...)` provide zero-API-call heuristics.
- **`budgets.py`** — `input_budget_for_config(config)`: returns `150_000` for Anthropic/claude-code, `100_000` for all other providers.
- **`validate.py`** — `validate_tool_input(value, schema)`: lightweight stdlib-only recursive JSON Schema validator (type, enum, min/max, required, properties, items). Returns human-readable error strings for LLM self-correction prompts.

---

### Public API Surface

The `__init__.py` barrel re-exports everything; canonical import pattern:

```python
from osoji.llm import (
    create_runtime,           # primary entry point for JunkAnalyzers
    LLMProvider,              # ABC for type annotations
    CompletionOptions,        # request config
    CompletionResult,         # response type
    Message, MessageRole,     # message primitives
    ToolDefinition, ToolCall, # tool contracts
    TokenStats,               # usage stats (from LoggingProvider.stats)
    RateLimitMetadata,        # post-call rate-limit info
    PromptTooLargeError, ToolSchemaValidationError, RequiredToolCallError,
    create_provider,          # bare provider factory
    get_provider_spec,        # provider metadata lookup
    provider_names,           # list of valid provider strings
    estimate_tokens_offline,  # fast no-API estimate
)
```

---

### Key Data Flow

**Normal completion call:**
```
options = CompletionOptions(model=..., tools=[...], reservation_key="phase3")
result = await logging_provider.complete(messages, system, options)
# LoggingProvider → RateLimitedProvider.acquire(ticket) → LiteLLMProvider.complete()
#   → litellm.acompletion() → parse response → tool-validation retry if needed
#   → finalize_success(ticket) → attach RateLimitMetadata → return CompletionResult
# LoggingProvider accumulates TokenStats
```

**Tool validation retry (LiteLLMProvider / ClaudeCodeProvider):**
Both concrete providers implement an internal retry loop (up to `_MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS = 3`). On schema validation failure, error feedback is appended to the conversation and the call is retried. On `RequiredToolCallError` or `ToolSchemaValidationError`, the exception propagates to the caller.

**Rate limit auto-tuning:**
On first successful response, `RateLimitedProvider._auto_tune_from_headers()` reads `anthropic-ratelimit-*` or `x-ratelimit-limit-*` headers and calls `rate_limiter.update_limits()` to calibrate RPM/TPM buckets from actual API quotas.

---

### Important Patterns & Conventions

- **Provider-per-file pattern**: Each HTTP provider (`anthropic.py`, `google.py`, etc.) is a zero-logic `LiteLLMProvider` subclass. Adding a new provider requires a new file + a string constant + registration in `factory._PROVIDERS` and `registry._PROVIDER_SPECS`.
- **`claude-code` is special**: No `litellm_prefix`, no API key env var, uses subprocess instead of HTTP. Maps to `ClaudeCodeProvider` which has its own complete/retry/parse pipeline independent of `LiteLLMProvider`.
- **Lazy imports**: `litellm` is imported inside `LiteLLMProvider.__init__` (not at module load) to avoid circular imports and startup cost. `_is_retryable_error` in `RateLimitedProvider` also imports litellm inline.
- **`close()` invariant**: `LiteLLMProvider.close()` must NOT call `GLOBAL_LOGGING_WORKER.stop()` — this corrupts worker state across multiple `asyncio.run()` calls in the audit orchestrator.
- **`_field()` defensive accessor**: `LiteLLMProvider` never directly accesses attributes on API response objects; always uses `_field(obj, key, default)` which handles both dict and object response types.
- **Shared rate limiter**: `create_runtime` returns the `RateLimiter` alongside the provider so callers can inject it into subsequent `create_runtime` calls, sharing quota tracking across multiple provider instances in the same audit run.
- **Token counting tiers**: `RateLimitedProvider` tries three methods in order (explicit estimate → `TokenCounter` API → `estimate_tokens_offline`) with silent fallthrough on failure.

## `src/osoji/llm/base.py` — LLM Provider Abstract Base Class

### Purpose
Defines the `LLMProvider` ABC (L8–46) that all concrete LLM provider implementations must satisfy. This is the polymorphic interface used throughout the Osoji audit system to interact with language models.

### Key Symbol

**`LLMProvider`** (L8–46) — `ABC` subclass with three abstract members:
- **`name`** (L15–19): `@property @abstractmethod` — returns a string identifier for the provider (e.g., `"anthropic"`). Subclasses must implement as a `@property`.
- **`complete`** (L21–38): `async @abstractmethod` — primary inference call. Accepts:
  - `messages: list[Message]` — conversation history
  - `system: str | None` — optional system prompt
  - `options: CompletionOptions` — model selection, tools, temperature, etc.
  - Returns `CompletionResult` containing content, tool calls, and usage statistics.
- **`close`** (L40–46): `async @abstractmethod` — tears down any persistent connections (HTTP sessions, etc.). Must be called when the provider is no longer needed; consumers should use `try/finally` or async context managers.

### Dependencies
All three typed interfaces come from the sibling module `.types` (i.e., `src/osoji/llm/types.py`):
- `Message` — conversation turn shape
- `CompletionOptions` — model/tool/parameter configuration
- `CompletionResult` — structured response including content, tool calls, usage

### Architectural Role
Pure interface contract — no state, no logic. Concrete providers (e.g., Anthropic, OpenAI wrappers) implement this interface. The audit orchestrator and junk analyzers depend on `LLMProvider` polymorphically; they accept any conforming implementation without knowing the underlying SDK.

### Constraints / Invariants
- `complete` is `async`; callers must `await` it.
- `close` must be called for cleanup; there is no `__aenter__`/`__aexit__` defined here — callers are responsible for lifecycle management.
- `name` is a `@property`, not a plain attribute; subclasses must use `@property` to satisfy the abstract contract.

signature.purpose: Defines the LLMProvider abstract base class that all LLM provider implementations must conform to.

## Purpose

Provider-aware prompt budget selector. Returns a per-request input token ceiling based on the active LLM provider declared in `Config`.

## Constants

- **`ANTHROPIC_MAX_INPUT_TOKENS`** (L7): `150_000` — ceiling used when provider is `"anthropic"` or `"claude-code"`.
- **`DEFAULT_MAX_INPUT_TOKENS`** (L8): `100_000` — ceiling used for all other (unknown) providers.

## Function

### `input_budget_for_config(config: Config) -> int` (L11–16)

Inspects `config.provider`, normalises it (`.strip().lower()`), and dispatches:

| Provider value (normalised) | Return value |
|---|---|
| `"anthropic"` or `"claude-code"` | `150_000` |
| anything else (including `None`) | `100_000` |

**Guard on None**: `config.provider or ""` prevents `.strip()` errors when provider is unset (L14).

## Design Notes

- Callers should pass the result as an upper bound when constructing prompts; they are responsible for staying within the budget.
- `"claude-code"` is treated identically to `"anthropic"` — both map to the higher limit — reflecting that claude-code is an Anthropic-hosted endpoint.
- No dependency on the LLM client itself; purely a lookup helper, making it easily testable in isolation.

## Dependencies

- `Config` from `..config` — only `config.provider` (a string or `None`) is accessed.

signature.purpose: Returns a conservative per-request input token budget based on the active LLM provider declared in Config.

## Purpose

LLM provider implementation that routes completions through the locally installed **Claude Code CLI** (`claude -p`) rather than the Anthropic HTTP API, letting users leverage their Claude subscription instead of per-token API charges.

---

## Module-Level Constants (L26-L29)

| Name | Value | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `_MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS` | `3` | Max retries for tool call forcing/validation |
| `_DEFAULT_LLM_TIMEOUT` | `1200` | Default subprocess timeout in seconds |
| `logger` | `logging.getLogger(__name__)` | Module logger |

---

## `ClaudeCodeCLIError` (L32–L47)

`RuntimeError` subclass raised when the CLI subprocess returns a non-zero exit code or reports `is_error: true` in JSON output. Carries structured attributes: `exit_code: int`, `stderr: str`, `command: list[str]`. Message format: `"Claude Code CLI exited with code {exit_code}: {stderr}"`.

---

## `ClaudeCodeProvider` (L50–L410)

Concrete `LLMProvider` subclass. Core design: serialises the message list into a flat string prompt, invokes `claude -p --output-format json` as an async subprocess, and parses the JSON result into a `CompletionResult`.

### Constructor (L58–L68)

CLI path resolution order:
1. `claude_path` parameter
2. `OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH` environment variable
3. `shutil.which("claude")` — raises `RuntimeError` if none found

Stores result in `self._claude_path`.

### `name` property (L70–L72)

Returns the string `"claude-code"`.

### `complete` (L74–L138)

Primary public method. Implements `LLMProvider.complete`.

**Flow:**
1. Serialize messages to string via `_serialize_messages`.
2. Detect a forced tool via `_get_forced_tool` (only `tool_choice.type == "tool"` is handled).
3. If forced tool exists, inject structured-output instructions into prompt via `_inject_tool_description`.
4. Execute CLI via `_execute_and_parse`.
5. **Retry loop** (only when `forced_tool` is not None):
   - Missing tool call → append `_append_missing_tool_feedback`, retry.
   - Schema validation errors → append `_append_validation_feedback`, retry.
   - Max attempts (`_MAX_TOOL_VALIDATION_ATTEMPTS = 3`) exceeded → raise `RequiredToolCallError` or `ToolSchemaValidationError`.
   - Success → return aggregated `CompletionResult` with summed token counts.
6. If no forced tool, return result immediately after first call.

Token counts (`total_input`, `total_output`) are summed across all retry attempts and reported in the final result.

### `close` (L140–L141)

No-op async method satisfying the `LLMProvider` interface.

---

## Private Methods

### Message Serialization

**`_serialize_messages(messages)` (L147–L162):**
Single-user-message shortcut — returns raw content. Otherwise wraps each message with a `[User]`/`[Assistant]`/`[System]` label block joined by `\n\n`.

**`_content_to_str(content)` (L164–L177):**
Static. Converts `str | list[dict]` to plain string. Text-typed dict blocks use the `"text"` field; non-text blocks are JSON-serialized; non-dict elements are `str()`-coerced.

### Tool Helpers

**`_get_forced_tool(options)` (L183–L195):**
Static. Returns the `ToolDefinition` matching `options.tool_choice["name"]` when `tool_choice["type"] == "tool"`, else `None`. Searches `options.tools` list by name.

**`_inject_tool_description(prompt, tool)` (L197–L205):**
Static. Appends structured-output instruction paragraph to prompt. Instructs model to respond with JSON matching schema, no text outside JSON.

**`_append_missing_tool_feedback(prompt, tool)` (L207–L217):**
Static. Appends `[System feedback]` paragraph explaining the missing required tool call.

**`_append_validation_feedback(prompt, errors)` (L219–L228):**
Static. Appends `[System feedback]` paragraph listing each validation error as `- {tool_name}: {error}`.

**`_validate_tool_calls(tool_calls, options)` (L230–L246):**
Validates each `ToolCall.input` against the schema from `options.tools` (via `validate_tool_input`) and runs any `options.tool_input_validators`. Returns `dict[str, list[str]]` mapping tool names to error lists; empty dict means success.

### CLI Execution

**`_build_cli_args(system, options, forced_tool)` (L252–L283):**
Constructs the `claude` subprocess argument list. Fixed flags:
- `-p` — non-interactive print mode
- `--output-format json`
- `--no-session-persistence`
- `--strict-mcp-config`
- `--disable-slash-commands`
- `--settings '{"claudeMdExcludes": ["**/*"]}'` — suppresses CLAUDE.md files
- `--tools ""` — disables all MCP/built-in tools

Optional additions:
- `--system-prompt {system}` if system prompt provided
- `--model {model}` from `_map_model(options.model)` if model string is non-empty
- `--json-schema {schema}` if `forced_tool` is set — triggers structured output
- `--max-budget-usd {OSOJI_CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_BUDGET}` if env var set

**`_map_model(model)` (L285–L291):**
Static. Strips `"anthropic/"` or `"claude-code/"` prefix from model strings before passing to CLI.

**`_execute_cli(args, prompt)` (L293–L357):**
Async subprocess runner. Key behaviors:
- Reads `OSOJI_LLM_TIMEOUT` env var (default `_DEFAULT_LLM_TIMEOUT = 1200`).
- **Strips `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from subprocess env** (L302) to force OAuth/subscription auth.
- Sends `prompt` to stdin.
- On `asyncio.TimeoutError`/`TimeoutError`: kills process and raises `TimeoutError` with hint to set `OSOJI_LLM_TIMEOUT`.
- On non-zero exit code: extracts error detail from stderr, or falls back to parsing stdout JSON `"result"` field (L331–L336).
- On `is_error: true` in parsed JSON: raises `ClaudeCodeCLIError(exit_code=1, ...)`.
- Returns parsed `dict[str, Any]`.

**`_execute_and_parse(prompt, system, options, forced_tool)` (L359–L368):**
Thin orchestrator: calls `_build_cli_args` → `_execute_cli` → `_parse_response`.

**`_parse_response(data, forced_tool)` (L374–L410):**
Static. Extracts from CLI JSON:
- Token counts from `data["modelUsage"]` dict (`{model: {inputTokens, outputTokens}}`). Model name is taken from the first key.
- If `forced_tool` and `structured_output` is present: wraps as `ToolCall(id="cc_{uuid12}", name=forced_tool.name, input=structured_output)`. Non-dict `structured_output` silently becomes `{}`.
- `stop_reason` is **always hardcoded to `"end_turn"`** (L409) — the CLI JSON format does not expose stop reason.
- `model` defaults to `"claude-code"` if `modelUsage` is absent.
- Returns `CompletionResult(content=None if tool_calls else data["result"], ...)`.

---

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Used At | Default | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH` | L59 | None | Override path to `claude` binary |
| `OSOJI_LLM_TIMEOUT` | L298 | `1200` | Subprocess timeout in seconds |
| `OSOJI_CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_BUDGET` | L279 | None | Pass `--max-budget-usd` to CLI |
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | L302 | — | **Stripped** from subprocess env to force OAuth |

---

## Key Invariants

- Only `tool_choice.type == "tool"` is supported; multi-tool or auto-tool-choice modes are not implemented via CLI flags.
- Tool retry loop only activates when `forced_tool` is not None; free-form completions never retry.
- The CLI is always invoked with `--tools ""` — no MCP tool access is granted to the subprocess.
- `stop_reason` is always `"end_turn"` regardless of actual CLI stop condition.
- `structured_output` from the CLI must be a dict; non-dict values are silently replaced with `{}`.

---

## Dependencies

- **`LLMProvider`** from `.base` — abstract base class
- **`.types`** — `CompletionOptions`, `CompletionResult`, `Message`, `MessageRole`, `RequiredToolCallError`, `ToolCall`, `ToolDefinition`, `ToolSchemaValidationError`
- **`validate_tool_input`** from `.validate` — schema validation for tool inputs
- `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` — subprocess management
- `shutil.which` — CLI discovery

signature.purpose: LLM provider that wraps the Claude Code CLI for subscription-based completions with forced-tool retry logic.

No caller ever passes claude_path explicitly. The parameter is used only in a short-circuit or-expression on line 59 (`resolved = claude_path or os.environ.get('OSOJI_CLAUDE_PATH')`), meaning the claude_path branch of that expression is always falsy and the env-var fallback always takes effect.

Downstream validators will likely raise schema errors on the empty dict, triggering the retry loop rather than producing a clear error about unexpected CLI output shape.

@source-hash: 88ae1219cd8782ca
@impl-hash: d721a482f51f69c3

## Purpose

Factory module for constructing LLM provider instances. Provides two public factory functions: a bare provider builder and a middleware-wrapped provider builder (logging + optional rate limiting). Acts as the primary entry point for the rest of the codebase to obtain ready-to-use `LLMProvider` instances.

## Provider Registry

`_PROVIDERS` (L15–21): Internal `dict[str, type[LLMProvider]]` mapping normalized string keys to concrete provider classes:
- `"anthropic"` → `AnthropicProvider`
- `"claude-code"` → `ClaudeCodeProvider`
- `"google"` → `GoogleProvider`
- `"openai"` → `OpenAIProvider`
- `"openrouter"` → `OpenRouterProvider`

Keys in this dict must be **normalized** names (as returned by `normalize_provider_name` from `.registry`). Adding a new provider requires both adding the class import and inserting it here.

## Factory Functions

### `create_provider(name="anthropic") -> LLMProvider` (L24–36)
Bare provider creation. Calls `normalize_provider_name(name)` first, then performs a dict lookup in `_PROVIDERS`. Raises `ValueError` with the sorted list of valid provider names if the name is not found. Returns an undecorated provider instance (no logging, no rate limiting).

### `create_logging_provider(name, *, rate_limiter, verbose, default_model) -> LoggingProvider` (L39–59)
Builds a fully decorated provider. Wrapping order (innermost → outermost):
1. **Base provider** — resolved via `create_provider(normalized_name)`
2. **`RateLimitedProvider`** — inserted only if `rate_limiter is not None`; receives a `TokenCounter(provider=normalized_name, default_model=default_model)` for per-model token accounting
3. **`LoggingProvider`** — always applied as the outermost wrapper; `verbose` controls log verbosity

The `LoggingProvider` always sees the rate-limited interface when a limiter is present, meaning it logs at the outer boundary regardless of throttling delays.

## Middleware Stacking Pattern

```
create_logging_provider("anthropic", rate_limiter=rl, verbose=True)
  → LoggingProvider(
        RateLimitedProvider(AnthropicProvider(), rl, TokenCounter("anthropic")),
        verbose=True
    )
```

When `rate_limiter=None`, the stack collapses to `LoggingProvider(AnthropicProvider())`.

## Key Dependencies

| Import | Source | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `normalize_provider_name` | `.registry` | Canonicalizes name strings before lookup |
| `RateLimiter` | `..rate_limiter` | Passed through to `RateLimitedProvider` |
| `TokenCounter` | `.tokens` | Tracks token usage per model for rate limiting |
| `LoggingProvider` | `.logging` | Outermost wrapper, always applied |
| `RateLimitedProvider` | `.rate_limited` | Optional middleware, applied before logging |

## Invariants & Constraints
- `normalize_provider_name` must be called before any `_PROVIDERS` lookup; raw user input is not safe to look up directly.
- `default_model` parameter on `create_logging_provider` is forwarded only to `TokenCounter` (used when no model is specified per-call), not to the base provider itself.
- Default provider for both factory functions is `"anthropic"` (L24, L40).

signature.purpose: Factory functions that instantiate and optionally wrap LLM providers with logging and rate-limiting middleware.

## `src/osoji/llm/google.py` — Google Gemini Provider Wrapper

Minimal adapter that specialises `LiteLLMProvider` for Google Gemini by fixing the provider name to `"google"`. The entire implementation is a single `__init__` (L9-10) that delegates to `super().__init__("google")`.

### Class

**`GoogleProvider`** (L6-10) — public, concrete subclass of `LiteLLMProvider`. No additional methods or attributes beyond the constructor. All Gemini-specific routing, model-name construction, and API calls are handled by the parent class, which receives `"google"` as its provider discriminant.

### Design Pattern

Follows the same provider-per-file pattern used by other LLM integrations in the `osoji.llm` package. Each provider is a zero-logic subclass of `LiteLLMProvider` that supplies its provider string at construction time. This keeps provider registration trivially easy (add a file, add a class) while centralising all LiteLLM interaction in `LiteLLMProvider`.

### Dependency

Imports only `LiteLLMProvider` from the sibling module `.litellm_provider`. No stdlib or third-party imports — LiteLLM is used transitively through the parent class.

### Usage

Instantiate directly: `provider = GoogleProvider()`. All LLM call methods are inherited; callers interact with the standard `LiteLLMProvider` interface.

signature.purpose: Thin adapter that wires the Google Gemini LLM provider into the shared LiteLLM backend by passing 'google' as the provider identifier.
